title: Tantrāloka Volume 4 — Āhnikas 5-6: The Individual Means and the Temporal Means
subtitle: Dyczkowski translation (2023). Āṇavopāya in detail, SAUḤ and KHPHREṀ seed-mantras, the ten voids, the six blisses, and time as a path of realization.

summary.one_line: Abhinavagupta's Āhnikas 5-6, translated by Mark Dyczkowski (2023). Āhnika 5 details the Individual Means (Āṇavopāya) — the path of outer supports, the six blisses, the ten voids, the Trika Liṅga, t...
summary.scope: Covers Āhnika 5 (the individual means, meditation of the intellect, the six blisses, utterance through the vital breath, entry into the supreme principle, the ten voids, ten states, ten abodes, unstruck sound, the Trika Liṅga, the seed-mantras SAUḤ/KHPHREṀ/KṢMRYŪM); Āhnika 6 (time as means, the sixfold path in the breath, the eight movements of breath, Vāmā/Jyeṣṭhā/Raudrī, the projection of night/day/planets/zodiac into the breath, the cycle of 60 years, cosmic time cycles, the five prāṇa-vāyus, and the emergence of phonemes).

sources.label: Tantrāloka of Abhinavagupta, Volume 4 (Āhnikas 5-6), translated by Mark S.G. Dyczkowski (2023). Full PDF, 61 MB.

body.text: The six blisses (ṣaḍānanda) are the ascending stages of bliss experienced as the vital breath rises through the central channel. They are: (1) the bliss of emission (visargānanda) — the joy of creative expansion; (2) the bliss of the supreme state (paramānanda) — the stillness beyond expansion; (3) the bliss of the origin of emission (visargodbhavānanda) — the source from which all bliss arises; (4) the bliss of cessation (virāmānanda) — the joy of dissolution; (5) the bliss of union (melanānanda) — the joy of the conjunction of Śiva and Śakti; (6) the bliss of the equal state (samānānanda) — the great bliss of nondual awareness that pervades all states. These correspond to the six levels of the syllable OṂ and to the six cakras. (Āhnika 5, vv. 43-52ab, pp. 39-53)

The ten voids (daśaśūnya) are the ten stages of the dissolution of consciousness as it ascends through the subtle body. They are located at specific points in the body: (1-5) the five voids in the heart, throat, palate, between the eyebrows, and at the crown; (6-10) five further voids beyond the crown extending to the End of the Twelve (dvādaśānta). Each void is a level of increasing subtlety, where the yogī successively abandons identification with the gross body, the subtle body, the mind, the intellect, and finally the ego. Beyond the tenth void is the supreme state, which is no void at all but the plenum of consciousness. (Āhnika 5, vv. 90-100, pp. 113-133)

Three seed-mantras are given for absorption practice: SAUḤ is the seed of Parā (the supreme goddess of the Trika), the emission-seed (sṛṣṭi-bīja) that purifies by creative expansion and then reabsorption. KHPHREṀ is the seed of Kālasaṃkarṣiṇī, the withdrawal-seed (saṃhāra-bīja) that forces consciousness into the central channel and devours time. KṢMRYŪM is the seed of the third goddess, the supreme emission-seed of the Krama tradition. Together, the three seeds correspond to the three goddesses of the Trika: Parā (SAUḤ), Parāparā (KṢMRYŪM), and Aparā (KHPHREṀ). (Āhnika 5, vv. 141-155ab, pp. 192-205)

The Trika Liṅga is the subtle mark or sign by which the yogī recognizes the stages of the ascent. It is a threefold structure corresponding to the three goddesses: the lower liṅga (Aparā) is in the heart, associated with the objective world; the middle liṅga (Parāparā) is in the throat, associated with the means of knowledge; the upper liṅga (Parā) is at the crown, associated with pure subjectivity. The Trika Liṅga is the inner form of the Trika maṇḍala — it is the body of the yogī himself as the locus of the entire path. (Āhnika 5, vv. 113-128, pp. 149-169)

The instruments of yoga (karaṇa) are the six means by which the yogī accomplishes the ascent: (1) the vital breath (prāṇa) as the vehicle; (2) the mind (manas) as the director; (3) the intellect (buddhi) as the discriminator; (4) the phonemes (varṇa) as the energies; (5) the voids (śūnya) as the fields; (6) the blisses (ānanda) as the rewards. These six instruments correspond to the six cakras and are the tools of Āṇavopāya. They are not different from consciousness but are consciousness in its self-articulation as the path. (Āhnika 5, vv. 129ab-132ab, pp. 169-179)

Āhnika 6 presents the Temporal Means (Kālopāya) — the path of liberation through time. Time is not an obstacle to realization but a path: the cycles of breath, day and night, the lunar month, the year, the twelve-year cycle, and the sixty-year cycle are all projections of consciousness. By attending to time within the breath, the yogī transcends time. The fundamental insight is that all time — from the briefest moment (kṣaṇa) to the vast cosmic cycles (kalpa) — is contained within a single breath. The breath is the microcosmic clock that encodes all of cosmic temporality. (Āhnika 6, vv. 1-130, pp. 214-358)

The sixfold path (ṣaḍadhvan) is present within the breath itself. The path of the forces (kalādhvan) corresponds to the six cakras; the path of the principles (tattvādhvan) to the six phases of the breath; the path of the worlds (bhuvanādhvan) to the six external referents; the path of the phonemes (varṇādhvan) to the six classes of letters; the path of the mantras (mantrādhvan) to the six seed-syllables; the path of the words (padādhvan) to the six levels of speech. The yogī who breathes consciously traverses all six paths with every breath cycle. (Āhnika 6, vv. 28cd-36, pp. 248-253)

Vāmā, Jyeṣṭhā, and Raudrī are the three goddesses of the will (icchā), knowledge (jñāna), and action (kriyā) — corresponding to the three phases of the breath. Vāmā is the 'left' goddess, the power of will that governs inspiration; Jyeṣṭhā is the 'eldest' goddess, the power of knowledge that governs the suspension; Raudrī is the 'fierce' goddess, the power of action that governs expiration. Together, they are the three aspects of the one Śakti, and their play in the breath generates all of cosmic temporality. (Āhnika 6, vv. 56-59, pp. 267-269)

The entire cosmic calendar is projected within the breath. The twelve months correspond to twelve phases of the breath cycle; the signs of the zodiac are twelve locations in the subtle body through which the breath passes; the lunar month of 30 days corresponds to 30 complete breath cycles; eclipses correspond to specific breath retentions. The sixty-year cycle (bṛhaspati-cakra) is encoded in the complex interactions of the five prāṇas across 21,600 daily breaths. The yogī who knows this correlation controls time by controlling the breath. (Āhnika 6, vv. 114cd-179, pp. 340-391)

The five prāṇas are each associated with specific temporal cycles. Prāṇa (inhalation) governs the solar cycle; Apāna (exhalation) governs the lunar cycle; Samāna (the equalizing breath) governs the equinoxes and solstices; Udāna (the ascending breath) governs the ascension of the moon through the nakṣatras; Vyāna (the pervasive breath) governs the precession of the equinoxes. Each prāṇa has its own 'mouth' (aperture) in the subtle body through which consciousness enters and exits. The supreme, subtle, and gross emergence of the phonemes (varṇodaya) occurs through these apertures. (Āhnika 6, vv. 186-241ab, pp. 394-449)

Entry into the supreme principle (paratattvāntaḥpraveśa) is the culmination of Āṇavopāya. The yogī, having ascended through the voids, the blisses, and the levels of sound, enters into the supreme principle that is beyond all voids — the plenum of consciousness that is Śiva. This entry is not a movement in space but a recognition: the yogī realizes that the entire ascent was a movement within himself, and that he has always been the supreme principle. The entry is marked by the dissolution of the distinction between the meditator, the meditation, and the meditated. (Āhnika 5, vv. 74cd-86ab, pp. 92-111)

gaps: Detailed phoneme emergence theory from Āhnika 6; the three goddesses Vāmā/Jyeṣṭhā/Raudrī
Full details of the six blisses meditation; the ten abodes; the Trika Liṅga visualization

issues.title: Detailed phoneme emergence theory not extracted
issues.description: The varṇodaya section of Āhnika 6 contains a complex theory of how phonemes emerge from the breath at different levels of subtlety. This should be extracted in detail.

issues.title: Appendix on time cycles by Freedom Cole
issues.description: Volume 4 includes a substantial appendix (pp. 450-532) on the cycles of time in the cycle of breath by Freedom Cole. This material should be extracted and integrated.

versions.summary: Initial RO created from Tantrāloka Volume 4 (Dyczkowski, 2023). 11 passages covering the six blisses, ten voids, SAUḤ/KHPHREṀ/KṢMRYŪM seed-mantras, Trika Liṅga, instruments of yoga, Kālopāya, sixfold path in the breath, Vāmā/Jyeṣṭhā/Raudrī, cosmic time in breath, the five prāṇas, and entry into the supreme principle.

timeline.event: RO created in draft state from newly acquired Tantrāloka Volume 4 PDF (61 MB, ~38,456 lines text). This completes another missing volume in the Dyczkowski set.
